planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring or divide established plants; plant in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun. Montauk daisy forms a woody base over time and benefits from hard cutting back in early spring to prevent legginess. Without annual pruning, plants become open and floppy; cut back by one-half to two-thirds in late spring. Salt-tolerant and excellent for coastal gardens.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Montauk daisy earns its place in the garden by being among the very last perennials to bloom, producing large, pure-white, single daisy flowers with bright-yellow centers from September through frost — often into November — when almost no other daisy-family plants remain in bloom. The large, somewhat succulent, dark-green, spoon-shaped leaves are attractive throughout the growing season and the shrubby, woody-based habit gives the plant year-round structure. Native to the coastal areas of Japan, it is famous for its salt tolerance and thrives in sandy, well-drained, seaside conditions that challenge many other perennials. It naturalized along the eastern seaboard of North America and became particularly associated with Montauk Point, Long Island, where large colonies established on coastal bluffs. Annual hard cutting in spring is essential to maintain compact, floriferous form — neglected plants become open and leggy and produce fewer flowers. The cultivar Amelia is a compact selection with larger flowers.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; late ox-eye daisy spreads moderately by rhizomes and forms large colonies over time, particularly in moist conditions. It is a very tall plant (4-6 feet) and may need staking or be planted where it can lean against other tall plants for support.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained to moderately wet soil in full sun to part shade; late ox-eye daisy is the autumn counterpart of the familiar ox-eye daisy (Leucanthemum vulgare) — but while the common daisy blooms in May and June, this species from eastern Europe blooms in October and November, making it among the very latest-flowering perennials in the entire garden. The large, pure-white single daisy flowers with yellow centers are produced on tall, clumping, multi-stemmed plants from October until hard frost, providing pure white daisy color at a season when the garden is otherwise dominated by purples, oranges, and golds. The dark-green, narrow, toothed leaves are attractive throughout the growing season and the plants spread steadily to form impressive drifts. Native to moist meadows, stream margins, and forest edges in southeastern Europe from Hungary to Ukraine, it adapts well to garden conditions across zones 4-9 and is outstanding for naturalizing in moist areas where its spreading habit can develop freely.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; Zawadski's chrysanthemum is notably more heat- and humidity-tolerant than many chrysanthemums and succeeds in the Southeast better than most garden mums. Divide every 2-3 years to maintain vigor and prevent center die-out.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; Zawadski's chrysanthemum is the earliest-blooming species in the genus, flowering from August into September — considerably earlier than most garden chrysanthemums that bloom in October — and filling a valuable mid-to-late-summer gap. The flowers are single or semi-double daisies in shades of pale pink to rose-pink to nearly white, with a more informal, naturalistic quality than the large, pompom garden mums, more closely resembling small, elegant wild daisies. Native to Korea, northeastern China, Siberia, and Japan, it is fully cold-hardy to zone 3 — far hardier than tender garden mums sold as container plants in autumn — and reliably perennial as a clumping, non-invasive perennial. It is also notably more tolerant of summer heat and humidity than many Chrysanthemum species, making it better adapted to southeastern US gardens where common garden mums often fail as perennials. The cultivar Clara Curtis (pink) is the most widely grown selection.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in spring in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; woolly-stemmed sage is frost-tender and is damaged below 30 degrees F. In zones 9-11 it grows as a large, shrubby perennial; in colder zones it can be grown in large containers and overwintered frost-free. It is a large plant — allow 3-4 feet of space. Fertilize monthly to support its vigorous growth and abundant flowering.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to part shade; woolly-stemmed sage is a dramatically bold Brazilian salvia producing some of the most visually striking flower spikes in the genus. The entire plant is covered in a dense layer of russet-orange to tawny-brown woolly hairs — on the stems, leaf undersides, and calyx — that create a warm, velvety texture throughout. The flower spikes, which appear from late summer through frost, carry deep orange-red to crimson-scarlet tubular flowers from rusty-orange woolly calyces in dense, compact whorls, creating a warm, glowing effect unlike the typically blue or purple tones of most salvias. The large leaves are roughly textured and prominently veined, with hairy surfaces on both sides. At 4-6 feet tall and as wide, it is a commanding plant in the late-season border or tropical-style garden. It is powerfully attractive to hummingbirds throughout the bloom period. In zones 9-11 it grows into a large perennial shrub; elsewhere it is a dramatic container specimen.

planting.note: Divide established clumps every 3-4 years in early spring or plant container-grown plants in moderately fertile, well-drained to moist soil in full sun; shining coneflower is a tall, vigorous plant (5-7 feet) that may need staking in exposed positions or can be planted where surrounding tall plants provide support. The cultivar Herbstsonne (Autumn Sun) is the most widely grown selection and the standard bearer for late-summer yellow daisy perennials.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, well-drained to moist soil in full sun; shining coneflower is one of the tallest and most impressive of all coneflowers, reaching 5-7 feet in excellent conditions, with large, single daisy flowers from August through September that have prominent, distinctive green cone centers rather than the dark-brown central cones of the familiar black-eyed Susan. The large, golden-yellow ray petals are proportionally longer and more gracefully reflexed than most rudbeckias, giving the flowers an especially elegant, slightly drooping appearance. The cultivar Herbstsonne (German for Autumn Sun) is justly celebrated as one of the great late-summer perennials, producing masses of these large, yellow-and-green daisies on tall, self-supporting stems above broad, strong foliage that is healthy and disease-resistant throughout the growing season. Native to the southeastern US, it grows well in heat and humidity — better than many coneflowers in the southern states — while also being fully cold-hardy to zone 4. An outstanding back-of-border plant that provides scale and late-season color at a time when the garden needs it most.
